data.title: Astera Labs: Scalable Backbone of AI Infrastructure 2.0
data.description:
The Switzerland of Silicon: A Core Thesis on the Connectivity Bottleneck

The modern epoch of artificial intelligence is characterized by a fundamental shift in data center physics. While the preceding decade focused on the raw FLOPs (Floating-Point Operations per Second) provided by central and graphics processing units, the current bottleneck has shifted to the "Interconnect." As AI models transition from billions to trillions of parameters, the ability to move data between accelerators, memory pools, and networking switches has become the primary determinant of system efficiency. Astera Labs stands as the singular, high-execution leader in this niche, providing the "picks and shovels" that enable the hyperscale gold rush.

Astera Labs provides an Intelligent Connectivity Platform that unifies semiconductor-based hardware with a sophisticated software-defined architecture. The thesis for an investment in Astera Labs rests on its role as the "Switzerland" of the AI rack. Unlike proprietary ecosystems that lock customers into a single vendor's stack, Astera's solutions are designed to interoperate across NVIDIA GPUs, AMD Instinct accelerators, and custom silicon (ASICs) developed by hyperscalers like Amazon (Trainium/Inferentia) and Google (TPU). This neutrality ensures that no matter which processor architecture wins the AI race, Astera Labs captures the underlying connectivity tax.

The market currently misprices Astera Labs by underestimating the "dollar content expansion" per rack as the industry moves from AI Infrastructure 1.0 (discrete servers) to 2.0 (integrated rack-scale fabrics). A 2x outcome within 12 months is plausible because the company is on the precipice of a massive volume ramp for its Scorpio fabric switches, which command significantly higher average selling prices (ASPs) than its legacy retimer products. As these higher-value components integrate into the next generation of Blackwell and Rubin-class systems, revenue and earnings power are likely to exceed current consensus by a margin wide enough to trigger a significant valuation re-rating.

Deconstructing the Q3 2025 Financial Performance: A Paradigm of Operating Leverage

Astera Labs' performance in the third quarter of 2025 was a definitive statement of execution, characterized by a substantial beat on both revenue and profitability metrics. The company reported revenue of $230.6 million, representing a 104% increase year-over-year and a 20% sequential increase. This growth was not merely an artifact of market tailwinds but a result of new AI platform ramps that utilized multiple product families simultaneously.

Q3 2025 Earnings Performance and Consensus Comparison

The most salient takeaway from the earnings call was the expansion of non-GAAP operating margins to a record 41.7%. This level of profitability is remarkable for a company that is simultaneously growing its workforce by 60% year-over-year. It reveals a business model with immense operating leverage: once the R&D for a specific connectivity standard (like PCIe 6.0) is completed, the incremental cost of scaling that solution across thousands of hyperscale racks is minimal. Furthermore, the company maintained a non-GAAP gross margin of 76.4%, which, despite a slight year-over-year contraction due to a higher mix of hardware modules, remains at the top tier of the semiconductor industry, rivaling NVIDIA's own margin profile.

Near-Term Market Dynamics: The Scorpio Ramp and PCIe 6.0 Transition

The immediate catalyst for Astera Labs over the next two to four quarters is the transition from the Scorpio P-Series to the Scorpio X-Series fabric switches. While the Aries retimers have historically been the "workhorse" of the company, the Scorpio switches represent a strategic elevation in the value chain.

The Scorpio P-Series is architected for "scale-out" applications, managing mixed traffic between CPUs, GPUs, and NICs. In Q3 2025, the P-Series continued its volume ramp at a lead hyperscaler, with additional design wins across multiple other platforms. However, the real prize is the Scorpio X-Series, which is designed for "scale-up" applications, specifically GPU-to-GPU clustering. Management expects the X-Series to shift into high-volume production throughout 2026, which will likely drive a significant increase in "dollar content per rack". In the Q3 transcript, President Sanjay Gajendra noted that PCIe 6.0 solutions already contributed in excess of 20% of revenue, suggesting that the industry's adoption of higher-speed standards is moving faster than most analysts anticipated.

For Q4 2025, the company has guided for revenue between $245 million and $253 million, implying that the momentum remains strong heading into the new fiscal year. This guidance reflects continued strength in the Aries and Taurus portfolios, even as the newer Scorpio products begin their steep climb.

Long-Term Strategic Horizon: UALink, Photonics, and AI Infrastructure 2.0

Looking beyond 2026, Astera Labs is positioning itself to lead the "AI Infrastructure 2.0" era through its involvement in the Ultra Accelerator Link (UALink) consortium and its strategic acquisition of aiXscale Photonics.

UALink is an open industry standard designed to challenge NVIDIA's proprietary NVLink, allowing hyperscalers to create massive clusters of up to 1,024 accelerators from different vendors. As a founding member, Astera Labs is developing the silicon that will act as the physical fabric for these open systems. This is a massive long-term catalyst; if UALink becomes the standard for non-NVIDIA deployments (as many expect for AMD and custom ASIC clusters), Astera Labs will effectively own the connectivity layer for a significant portion of the total AI market.

Simultaneously, the acquisition of aiXscale Photonics, announced in October 2025, addresses the looming physical limits of copper. As data rates push toward 1.6T and 3.2T per link, traditional electrical signaling over copper cables will suffer from excessive heat and signal degradation. aiXscale's technology in fiber-chip coupling allows Astera to develop optical interconnects, which provide higher bandwidth and lower power consumption over longer distances. This move secures Astera's relevance in a future where optical connectivity is mandatory for rack-to-rack communication.

Valuation Anchor: Quantifying the Intrinsic Value and Mispricing

Astera Labs currently trades at a significant premium to the broader semiconductor sector, which has led some traditional value investors to avoid the stock. However, a nuanced analysis of its forward multiples against historical averages and peer performance suggests that the stock remains undervalued relative to its growth potential.

Comparative Valuation Multiples

The mispricing of ALAB stems from the "Multiple Compression Myth." While a forward P/E of 88x appears high, it is a sharp reduction from its post-IPO average of 144.5x and its historical peak of over 160x. If the company maintains its 54.6% projected 3-to-5 year EPS growth rate, the valuation multiple will compress rapidly even as the stock price rises. For example, the 2-year forward P/E is already projected to drop to 69.1x, and the 3-year forward to 50.9x.

To justify a 2x outcome (a price target of ~$325), the company needs to reach an annual EPS of approximately $4.00 by 2027 while maintaining a 75x-80x multiple. This is plausible if the Scorpio fabric switches capture 20% of the non-NVIDIA scale-up market, effectively tripling the company's current revenue base. The intrinsic value, derived from a 10-year DCF model using a 12% discount rate and a 4% terminal growth rate, yields a fair value in the $200 range today, but this model often fails to capture the "option value" of new standards like UALink and Photonics, which could extend the high-growth phase indefinitely.

Strategic Catalysts: Triggers for the Next Twelve Months

Several specific events over the next year could unlock substantial value for shareholders:

Scorpio X-Series Design Wins: Official announcements of the X-Series being integrated into the next-gen AI platforms of Google or Meta would validate the company's "scale-up" strategy and lead to significant estimate revisions.

Hyperscaler Capex Resilience: Continued guidance from Microsoft, AWS, and Google for over $500 billion in AI-related Capex in 2026 would provide the macro backdrop for sustained demand.

UALink 1.0 Specification Release and Silicon Tape-Out: As the first real alternative to NVLink, any progress on the UALink standard is a direct tailwind for Astera, as it is the primary silicon enabler for this anti-NVIDIA coalition.

NVLink Fusion Integration: Deepening the partnership with NVIDIA to provide "semi-custom" connectivity solutions for Blackwell systems ensures that Astera benefits even from NVIDIA's continued dominance.

CXL Memory Controller Ramp (Leo): As inference becomes a larger part of the AI workload, the demand for expanded memory via CXL will rise. Leo is uniquely positioned to address the "Memory Wall" at Azure and AWS.

The Bear Case: Why the Stock Might Not Double

The primary risk to the bull case is "Priced for Perfection." At current levels, any delay in product ramps or a slight margin miss could result in a 20-30% pullback.

Competitive Encroachment: Broadcom and Marvell are not standing still. Broadcom's recent focus on PCIe retimers and Marvell's leadership in active copper cables pose a direct threat to Astera's market share.

Customer Concentration: Astera Labs is heavily dependent on a few large customers. If a major hyperscaler decides to develop an in-house connectivity solution to save on margins, Astera's TAM would shrink overnight.

Gross Margin Pressure: As the company shifts toward selling more hardware modules (like Taurus SCMs) versus pure silicon, gross margins could face downward pressure, disappointing investors who are paying a premium for 75%+ margins.

Macro Volatility: A broader downturn in AI spending or a "digestion phase" in hyperscaler Capex would hit high-beta names like ALAB disproportionately.

Guru Activity and Institutional Sentiment

The ownership structure of Astera Labs provides a window into "Smart Money" sentiment. Institutional ownership has increased to 72.6% as of January 2026, indicating that professional money managers are increasingly comfortable with the long-term story.

Institutional Flows and Guru Activity

While institutional buying has been aggressive, insider activity has been dominated by planned selling. Director Manuel Alba sold shares worth approximately $24.9 million in December 2025, and CEO Jitendra Mohan sold over $26 million in shares in November. However, it is important to note that these sales are often conducted under 10b5-1 plans and represent a small fraction of their total holdings, with insiders still owning roughly 12.5% of the company. Furthermore, the lack of "Strong Sell" ratings from analysts, with 17 "Buy" and 5 "Hold" ratings, suggests that the technical and fundamental community remains broadly supportive.

Capital Allocation and Execution Strategy

Astera Labs' management has demonstrated a disciplined approach to capital allocation, prioritizing R&D and strategic M&A over buybacks or dividends. With $1.13 billion in cash and zero debt, the company is in a position of extreme strength.

The decision to acquire aiXscale Photonics rather than attempting to build an optical division from scratch shows a "buy vs. build" pragmatism that accelerates their time-to-market for AI Infrastructure 2.0. Additionally, the aggressive hiring of engineering talent in Vietnam, Singapore, and Silicon Valley suggests that the company is building a global moat that will be difficult for smaller competitors to replicate. Their COSMOS software suite acts as a further "stickiness" factor, as customers who integrate Astera's telemetry and management tools into their data center operations are unlikely to switch to a different hardware provider for their next cluster upgrade.

Conclusion: A High-Conviction "Buy" for the Infrastructure Era

Astera Labs is not just another chip company; it is the essential fabric of the generative AI revolution. By focusing on the physical layer of data movement, it has carved out a niche with 76% gross margins and triple-digit revenue growth. The current valuation, while high on a trailing basis, fails to account for the massive earnings power that will be unlocked as the Scorpio X-Series and UALink products hit volume production in 2026 and 2027.

For the investor willing to look past near-term multiple volatility, Astera Labs offers a unique opportunity to own a critical piece of the AI value chain that is agnostic to which GPU or TPU ultimately wins the compute wars. Its high level of operating leverage, robust balance sheet, and strategic foresight in photonics make it one of the most compelling growth stories in the technology sector. While the bear case warns of competition and concentration, Astera's relative execution and position as the "Switzerland" of connectivity provide a buffer that justifies its premium status. A strategic allocation at these levels targets the inevitable re-rating that occurs when a high-growth leader transitions from a niche component provider to a dominant infrastructure platform.

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: Ministry of Economy and Tourism and Emirates Growth Fund sign MoU to boost SME growth and accelerate global market access
data.description:
Partnership to enhance the role of SMEs in the development of UAE's economic clusters

Abu Dhabi: The Ministry of Economy and Tourism (MoET) signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the Emirates Growth Fund (EGF) to strengthen the role of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in the development of the UAE’s economic clusters, support their sustainable growth, and accelerate their access to regional and international markets. The MoU focuses on empowering SMEs, fostering innovation, and launching advanced accelerator programmes that enhance their capacity to scale and expand. This collaboration aims to increase SMEs’ active contribution to the national economy and reinforce the UAE’s position as a leading global destination for entrepreneurship.

The signing ceremony was attended by H.E. Abdulla bin Touq Al Marri, Minister of Economy and Tourism and Chairman of the UAE Council for Entrepreneurship; and H.E. Abdullah Ahmed Al Saleh, Undersecretary of the Ministry of Economy and Tourism. The MoU was signed on behalf of the Ministry by H.E. Dr. Maria Hanif Alqassim, Assistant Undersecretary for the Policies & Economic Studies sector, while H.E. Najla Ahmed Al Midfa, Vice Chairperson of EGF’s Board of Director, was representing the EGF, and Khalifa Al Hajeri, Chief Executive Officer of EGF, signed the MoU on behalf of EGF.

H.E. Bin Touq affirmed that the UAE continues to strengthen its global position as a leading hub for entrepreneurship through pioneering national initiatives, infrastructure development, and adoption of advanced technologies that support entrepreneurs, talents, innovators, and owners of startups and innovative investment ideas.

H.E. said: “Under the guidance of the wise leadership, the UAE has prioritized the development of economic clusters in key sectors, in line with the National Policy for Economic Clusters, while also strengthening the role of startups and small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in advancing these clusters and increasing their contribution to building a knowledge-based economic model. In this context, the signing of the MoU with the Emirates Growth Fund represents a new step in supporting this national direction by empowering SMEs and enhancing their capacity to expand in new economy sectors. This cooperation contributes to advancing economic diversification efforts, stimulating innovation, improving the efficiency of economic clusters, and strengthening their competitiveness at both regional and global levels.”

H.E. Najla Ahmed Al Midfa, Vice Chair of the Emirates Growth Fund, said: “The Emirates Growth Fund is addressing one of the most persistent funding gaps: access to growth capital for high-potential SMEs. As a long-term partner, we combine patient capital with active value creation. Our partnership with the Ministry of Economy and Tourism further strengthens our ability to help these SMEs scale sustainably, compete globally, and build institutions that contribute enduring value to a diversified and competitive UAE economy.”

Khalifa Al Hajeri, CEO of the Emirates Growth Fund, added: “This MoU marks another milestone in EGF’s mission to unlock the growth potential of small and medium-sized enterprises, while strengthening their readiness to scale locally and globally. Through this collaboration, we are seeking opportunities for us to invest growth equity in companies built to last, supporting them throughout their growth journey by providing strategic, long-term capital aligned with national economic priorities, and reinforcing the role of SMEs as a key driver of the UAE’s economic growth.”

The MoU aims to provide specialized capacity-building programmes for SMEs, encourage them to innovate and accelerate their business growth, as well as facilitate access to local and international markets and support expansion plans. It also focuses on providing diverse investment and financing opportunities for these companies.

The partnership contributes to achieving the UAE’s national targets for the SME sector and supporting its expansion into new and sustainable economy sectors. It also enables both parties to implement joint initiatives and projects that enhance the sector’s contribution to the national economy. The MoU also stipulates the exchange of knowledge and expertise between the Ministry of Economy and Tourism and the EGF in areas related to investment, which contributes to enhancing business sustainability in the country.

MoU between EGF and UAE Food Cluster

H.E. Bin Touq also witnessed the signing of another Memorandum of Understanding between the EGF and the UAE Food Cluster. The MoU was signed by Khalifa Al Hajeri, CEO of the EGF on behalf of the Fund, and Saleh Lootah, Chairman of the Board of Directors of the UAE Food Cluster.

The MoU will enable EGF to access companies under the UAE Food Cluster, and to provide them with strategic growth capital supported by the creation of real value. This contributes to achieving the UAE’s food security goals, supporting the diversification of the national economy, and building a more self-sufficient and sustainable food ecosystem within the country.

data.title: Wall Street banks' fourth-quarter earnings in five charts
data.description:
By Manya Saini

Wall Street's largest banks ended 2025 on a strong note and executives struck an upbeat tone for the year ahead, even as U.S. President Donald Trump's proposed cap on credit card interest rates kept the industry on edge.

Analysts expect high-profile IPOs and big-ticket deals to sustain momentum for investment banking, while persistent market volatility is set to keep trading strong.

Below are key trends from the fourth-quarter results of top U.S. banks:

SPOTLIGHT ON CONSUMER BANKING

Interest income, a measure of lending profitability, rose across the big banks, supported by healthy loan growth and a decline in deposit costs.

Commercial and industrial loans and credit card balances edged higher in the quarter as borrowing demand held up despite elevated interest rates.

Trump's proposed 10% cap on credit card interest rates drew a sharp pushback from the industry this week, with executives warning it would restrict credit availability for everyday Americans and weigh on the economy.

Credit cards are among the most profitable products as they carry some of the highest interest rates since the debt is unsecured.

Credit quality remains a top focus as analysts and investors scrutinized the results for signs of strain in consumer and business health.

Charge-offs, or debt that a bank declares as unlikely to be paid back, reflect how consumers are faring and the state of household finances.

INVESTMENT BANKING STRENGTH

Mergers and acquisitions rebounded strongly in 2025 and pushed global investment banking revenues above $100 billion, according to data from Dealogic.

Bankers are optimistic about another robust year as antitrust headwinds ease, markets hover near record levels and the U.S. economy remains resilient.

TRADING MOMENTUM

Banks capitalized on market swings, generating revenue from client portfolio rebalancing and increased proprietary trading. Many also benefited from a surge in demand for products that protect against market swings.

"Equity trading revenues have been the story of the earnings so far, strong gains in the use of leverage/options were big drivers of that growth," said Brian Mulberry, senior client portfolio manager at Zacks Investment Management, which owns several large-cap banking stocks.

Volatility is expected to persist in 2026 amid concerns about stretched valuations, a bubble in AI stocks and uncertainty over the Federal Reserve's policy path.

STOCK PERFORMANCE

"Overall, we are bullish on the big banks as the economy remains stable, labor market is a little softer but not as weak as feared and with stable prices consumers are still spending," Mulberry said.

data.title: Air India, Singapore Airlines to deepen ties with 'cooperation framework'
data.description:
Air India and Singapore Airlines on Friday announced an agreement to improve cooperation between the two flag carriers, a step towards offering more routes, coordinating schedules and broadening the scope of their collaboration outside their home markets.

The pact, described by the two airlines as a "commercial cooperation framework agreement", comes as Air India faces a financial hit from an airspace ban imposed by Pakistan, which has forced the airline to discontinue some routes to the U.S. from India.

The airlines said the agreement could include expanding both carriers’ corporate travel programmes, a key source of high-yield revenue for airlines.

The pact is subject to regulatory approvals and signing of definitive agreements, the airlines added.

Air India already has a codesharing agreement with Singapore Airlines, which owns a 25% stake in the company. Tata Group owns the remaining 75%.

data.title: TSX futures rise as oil steadies, Canada PM announces China trade deal
data.description:
Futures tracking Canada's main stock index edged higher on Friday, as oil prices regained some lost ground and Prime Minister Mark Carney secured an initial trade deal with China.

March futures on Toronto's S&P/TSX Composite Index (SXFcv1) were up 0.24% as of 5:42 a.m. ET.

Canada and China have agreed to cut tariffs on electric vehicles and canola, Carney said on Friday, with both nations promising to tear down trade barriers while forging new strategic ties.

China is the world's largest canola seed buyer and halted imports from Canada last year amid a diplomatic dispute, tightening supplies and bringing its canola crushing industry to a standstill. China was a $4 billion canola seed market for Canada, according to Carney.

The benchmark index closed at a record high on Thursday, as rising financial and industrial shares outweighed losses in commodity-linked stocks, which were dragged down by falling crude oil and precious metal prices.

But oil firmed on Friday, with traders assessing supply concerns, even as chances of a U.S. strike on Iran receded. Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude rose more than 1%, after a 4% drop on Thursday.

Spot gold and silver both extended the previous session's losses as stronger-than-expected economic data from the U.S. tempered expectations for near-term Federal Reserve rate cuts.

On the macro front, investors await December inflation data for Canada due on January 19.

data.title: Applied Digital Corporation: Doubled Revenue Visibility in AI Data Centers
data.description:
Stock Idea: APLD

Applied Digital Corporation builds and runs next-generation data centers tailored for AI and high-performance computing workloads, with massive leases already in place from players like CoreWeave . At around $37-38 per share today, the stock trades at roughly 40x next-twelve-months sales despite explosive revenue growth and $11 billion in locked-in lease revenueway below AI infrastructure peers commanding 10-20x sales on similar trajectories. Doubling to $75+ within a year makes sense as data center expansions come online, ramping recurring cash flows in the heart of the AI boom.

Thesis

APLD designs, constructs, and operates digital infrastructure for HPC and AI, including GPU cloud services under Sai Computing and hosting for crypto miners pivoting to AI. The stock looks mispriced because Wall Street fixates on current losses from heavy capex, ignoring the $11B in long-term leases (like the 150MW CoreWeave deal) that guarantee revenue visibility while shares trade at a discount to hyperscaler-adjacent names. A 2x move by January 2027 is realistic: Q4 revenue hit $127M (up 98% YoY), adjusted EBITDA crushed at $20M, and phased data center deliveries could triple capacity, fueling FCF inflection amid AI power demand surging.

Valuation Anchor

Enterprise value sits around $11B on TTM revenue of $264M, but forward sales could hit $500M+ from leases, implying an EV/sales multiple of 22xstill cheap versus AI data center peers like Digital Realty or Equinix at 30-40x on slower growth. Anchor to contracted leases: $11B over 12-15 years at 20-25% EBITDA margins (recent quarters show 16% adjusted) pencils to $2B+ annual run-rate EBITDA by 2027, justifying EV/EBITDA of 5-6x today versus peers' 15-20x, supporting $20B+ EV or $75/share. Losses are capex-driven; FCF turns positive as assets activate, mirroring how SMCI or VRT multiples expanded on execution.

Catalysts

  • Polaris Forge expansions: 400MW+ with CoreWeave/Polaris Forge 2 online in 2026, adding $4B+ revenue.
  • Cloud spin-off (ChronoScale): Unlocks value via 97% retained stake, focusing core on AI hosting.
  • Earnings beats and financing: Recent $5B+ capital raises fuel builds; Q1/Q4 crushes signal more.
  • AI hyperscaler demand: Broader leases as Nvidia GPU ramps strain power supply.

Bear Case

Heavy debt from expansions ($3B+ EV overhang) could spike if interest rates stay high or equity dilutes furtherrecent raises already bloated shares outstanding. Tenant concentration (CoreWeave ~40% capacity) risks defaults or AI slowdowns, leaving idle assets and negative FCF persisting ($128M TTM loss). Competition from giants like Microsoft-owned infra could cap pricing power, stalling multiples below 10x sales if execution slips on power/cooling delays. A broader market pullback would hit APLD hard too, as we're deep into a multi-year bull and any weakness drags high-beta names like this down regardless of fundamentals.

Guru Activity

Recent insider activity shows sales from Director Jason Zhang Miller (3,085 shares sold Oct 2024 at ~$9.60, valued ~$29K) and Director Wes Cummins Higgins (1,000 shares sold Sep 2024 at ~$9.50, valued ~$9K), likely profit-taking or liquidity amid the run-up.

Institutions have been active in APLD, with notable guru moves adding conviction despite some churn. George Soros (Trades, Portfolio) initiated a new position of 830,000 shares (0.13%) at Q4 2024-end when shares traded around $8.31, then fully sold out by Q1 2025-end near $7.85suggesting tactical trading around volatility. Ken Fisher (Trades, Portfolio) bought 16,000 shares back in mid-2022 at $4.86 but sold out entirely by late 2024 near $8.31, locking in solid gains as the stock tripled-plus. Jefferies Group (Trades, Portfolio) bought 58,000 shares in Q3 2024 at $5.30 levels before exiting by year-end, another short-term play amid the rally.

Conclusion

Applied Digital Corporation sits at the epicenter of AI's power crunch, with leases locking in billions while the market obsesses over buildout costs. Execution on Polaris Forge and beyond could flip this from capex sink to cash machine, easily warranting that 2x pop even after the gurus trimmed. Bears have valid shots on debt and concentration, but in this AI freight train, APLD's risk-reward skews hard to the upside for patient bulls.

data.title: Microsoft's Activision Blizzard Probed by Italian Regulator Over In-Game Purchases
data.description:
By Edith Hancock

Italy's competition regulator is investigating Microsoft's Activision Blizzard, saying the U.S. gaming publisher might be misleading consumers through its popular games "Diablo Immortal" and "Call of Duty Mobile".

The Italian Competition Authority, or AGCM, said Friday that Activision --acquired by Microsoft in 2023 for $69.7 billion--might have breached Italian consumer protection laws by using misleading and aggressive practices to nudge users to play more often, extend their gaming sessions and take up promoted offers. It said the games are described as free to play but still offer in-game purchases.

Neither Microsoft nor Activision Blizzard immediately responded to requests for comment.

The investigation will hone in on repeated prompts that urge users not to miss out on rewards or purchase time-limited offers, the watchdog said. "These practices, together with strategies that make it difficult for users to understand the real value of the virtual currency used in the game and the sale of in-game currency in bundles, may influence players as consumers - including minors," the AGCM said, adding that it can lead players to spend significant sums without being fully aware of the real costs.

The watchdog said parental controls for the games appear to default to a less restrictive version that enables minors to make in-game purchases. The AGCM said it would also look at how the games ask for consent for personal data processing and whether gamers get enough information about their contractual rights.

Microsoft faced a rocky regulatory process when it bought Activision as global merger watchdogs screened the deal for potential competition issues. The transaction was cleared by the European Commission--tasked with investigating multinational corporations' multi-billion euro transactions--after the companies offered to license Activision games to rival platforms for 10 years and let users stream Activision games through rival cloud steaming services.

It was also initially blocked by the U.K.'s competition regulator, but later cleared after the companies restructured it to include selling Activision's cloud streaming rights to France's Ubisoft.

Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com

data.title: Deutsche Bank Results Likely to Be Uneventful — Market Talk
data.description: Deutsche Bank's fourth-quarter results should be relatively quiet after the lender gave an update on its midterm plans in November, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods's Thomas Hallett and Andrew Stimpson say in a research note. "Management is expected to provide additional detail on 2026 targets and reaffirm its constructive outlook--neither of which should come as a surprise," the analysts say. Deutsche Bank will likely guide for revenue of 33 billion euros and a return on tangible equity--a key profitability metric--of 10.5%, while announcing plans for 1.9 billion euros in dividends and a 1.1 billion-euro buyback, according to KBW. The current environment looks supportive for Deutsche Bank, but its heavy reliance on investment banking and exposure to risks are reasons to be cautious, the analysts say. Shares fall 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Dollar Could Fall Against Most Currencies This Year — Market Talk
data.description: The dollar looks set to depreciate against most currencies this year as lower U.S. interest rates make it cheaper to take protection against the risk of the currency weakening, Bank of America analysts say in a note. With U.S. rate differentials set to narrow further as the Federal Reserve cuts rates, the costs of hedging U.S. assets against a weaker dollar will also fall, they say. "We see still relatively high hedging costs as one of the key hurdles that may have prevented follow-through of hedging activity (of dollar exposures) in 2025." The Trump administration's push to lower housing related costs could encourage further rate cuts, they say. Fed independence risks also pose a potential dollar headwind. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Stock Idea: Zevra Therapeutics
data.description:
1) Clear Thesis

What they do: Zevra is a commercial-stage rare disease company. Its flagship drug, Miplyffa

(approved Sept 2024 for NPC), is now generating meaningful revenue, complemented by a massive

cash reserve.

Why it's mispriced: The market still treats ZVRA as a cash-burning biotech, ignoring its financial

transformation. With $230M in cash (boosted by a $150M PRV sale in 2025) and Miplyffa

generating ~$26M in Q3 2025 alone, the company is on the cusp of profitability. The stock

currently trades at just ~2 cash.

Path to 2: As Zevra reports its first profitable quarters in 2026, the valuation will re-rate from a

"cash shell" discount to a growth multiple (3-5 sales), driving the price toward $18+.

2) Valuation Anchor

Metric: EV/Sales Re-rate

Run Rate: Annualizing Q3 2025 revenue ($26M) suggests a $100M+ revenue baseline.

The Math: A conservative 3 EV/Sales multiple on $100M revenue = $300M Enterprise

Value.

Target: $300M EV + $230M Net Cash = $530M Market Cap (~$10/share floor). Growth

to peak sales projections supports analyst targets of $26.00 (almost 3 upside).

3) Catalysts (Next 12 Months)

Profitability Inflection (Q4 2025/Q1 2026): Analysts forecast positive EPS ($0.05) in

upcoming quarters. Confirming this transition will eliminate dilution risk and attract

fundamental investors.

EU Expansion: Recent distribution deals signal European market entry, unlocking a second

major revenue stream beyond the U.S.

4) Bear Case

Competitive Threat: IntraBio's Aqneursa also treats NPC. If Miplyffa loses significant

market share to this competitor, revenue growth could stall.

Execution Risk: Any stumble in the commercial rollout of Miplyffa or Olpruva could delay

the profitability timeline, burning through the cash buffer.

5) Guru Activity

Insider Buying (Late 2025): Directors Alvin Shih (bought ~20k shares) and John Bode

(bought ~5k shares) recently added to their positions.

Smart Money: Nantahala Capital Management increased its stake by ~34% in Q3 2025,

signaling high conviction.

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: Huawei appoints Rajesh Nagpal as Vice President of Enterprise Business for UAE
data.description:
Dubai, United Arab Emirates: Huawei, a global leader in information and communications technology (ICT) infrastructure and smart devices, has announced the appointment of Rajesh Nagpal as Vice President of Enterprise Business for Huawei UAE, reinforcing the company’s continued investment in local leadership and its commitment to advancing enterprise digital transformation across the country.

Rajesh will lead Huawei’s Enterprise Business in the UAE, overseeing strategy, sales execution, and ecosystem development to support customers across key sectors including government, energy, financial services, transportation, and large enterprises. He will focus on expanding Huawei’s enterprise portfolio, strengthening partner collaboration, and enabling customers to adopt advanced cloud, data, AI, and networking solutions aligned with national digital priorities.

Rajesh brings more than two decades of experience in enterprise technology and business leadership, with a strong track record of driving large-scale transformation programs and building high-performing teams. Prior to joining Huawei, he held senior roles at Gulf Business Machines (GBM), where he partnered with CXOs and senior business leaders to translate technology investments into tangible business outcomes and long-term value.

Commenting on the appointment, David Tao, CEO of Huawei UAE, said: “We are pleased to welcome Rajesh Nagpal to Huawei. His deep understanding of the enterprise market and proven leadership across complex customer environments will play an important role as we continue to strengthen our enterprise business in the UAE. This appointment reflects our commitment to developing local leadership and supporting customers on their digital and intelligent transformation journeys.”

Highlighting his enthusiasm to join the Huawei team, Rajesh Nagpal said: “Stepping into this role at Huawei comes at a time when organisations across the UAE are accelerating their digital transformation journeys. Huawei has built a strong foundation in the UAE’s enterprise market through deep customer relationships and a robust partner ecosystem. I look forward to building on this momentum, working closely with stakeholders to help organisations modernize their operations and unlock new opportunities through digital and intelligent technologies.”

Under Rajesh’s leadership, Huawei Enterprise Group will continue to work closely with customers and partners in the UAE to accelerate industrial intelligence and support digital and intelligent economic development, helping create future-ready digital infrastructure across key industries. 

About Huawei

Founded in 1987, Huawei is a leading global provider of information and communications technology (ICT) infrastructure and smart devices. We have more than 208,000 employees, and we operate in more than 170 countries and regions, serving more than three billion people around the world.

Our vision and mission is to bring digital to every person, home and organization for a fully connected, intelligent world. To this end, we will drive ubiquitous connectivity and promote equal access to networks; bring cloud and artificial intelligence to all four corners of the earth to provide superior computing power where you need it, when you need it; build digital platforms to help all industries and organizations become more agile, efficient, and dynamic; redefine user experience with AI, making it more personalized for people in all aspects of their life, whether they're at home, in the office, or on the go.
